title: Why we do not need fluoride to prevent tooth decay

chunks.title: Introduction to Cavities and Diet
chunks.summary: The speaker introduces the idea that cavities are not simply a result of fluoride deficiency but rather can be prevented through an animal-based diet. They reference the work of Weston Price and suggest that fat-soluble vitamins are crucial for dental health.
chunks.transcript: i do not believe cavities are a fluoride deficiency if you look at the work of western price and the diets of religious people i think it's very clear that humans can avoid cavities by eating an animal-based diet i did a podcast a couple years ago with a biologic dentist and we talked about the odontoblast the immune cells in the teeth that are involved in breaking down or fighting against the enamel damage that is being caused by bacteria when you have an acidic environment in the saliva and teeth after eating sugar most of the mainstream media would believe that sugar causes tooth decay and i think that is true if you are fat soluble vitamin deficient and if you're immune cells in your teeth yes your teeth are living yes your teeth contain immune cells called odontoblast if those immune cells are not adequately nourished with fat soluble vitamins what

chunks.title: Personal Experience with Cavities
chunks.summary: The speaker shares their personal experience with cavities while adhering to a vegan diet, noting that they had multiple cavities at one point but now have none after changing their dietary habits. They emphasize the connection between diet, especially fat-soluble nutrients, and tooth health.
chunks.transcript: sugar when i was a vegan i was eating food every day and i went to the dentist and they told me i had seven cavities i didn't feel them at the time i said uh i don't wanna get those filled i didn't know that i was gonna not be a vegan in a few months but guess what i have zero cavities now so clearly changing my diet was a major thing for me and those cavities did not progress and i eat a massive amount of fruit right now if you've seen me in my stories i do not fear fruit but you know for fructose i've talked about that on previous podcasts at length so i do not believe that fruit is the only variable in the formation of tooth cavities i believe that is a connection or a contextual variable with the fat soluble vitamin deficiency and we see that hypothesis corroborating in studies of hunter-gatherer tribes and indigenous people who are eating lots of fat soluble vitamins vitamin k2 was western prices x factor this fat soluble nutrient found in butter egg yolks liver raw milk etc that i think is the key to a healthy mouth now when i was growing up did i have a lot of that nope wasn't because my mom and dad wanted to deprive me of it they just didn't understand the value of those foods in a child's life i understand the value of those foods in a child's life and if i ever have kids you can better believe my kids will basically be swimming in egg yolks liver meat meat fat and probably some butter and hopefully they'll be pretty happy and healthy kids and i won't fear giving them honey from local raw sources or fruit and i do not believe they will get cavities that's an experiment to be done in the future i will keep you guys posted but i think that is an experiment that has already been worn out multiple times in human populations and we have seen the results clearly uh it's certainly an experiment that i'm doing right now myself and i don't have cavities and i eat a lot of fat soluble nutrients and a lot of

chunks.title: Nutrient Sources for Dental Health
chunks.summary: The discussion focuses on sources of fat-soluble vitamins vital for dental health, such as vitamin D, A, E, K2, and animal fats. The speaker highlights the importance of these nutrients in a child's diet and expresses a commitment to providing them to future children.
chunks.transcript: am i talking about here vitamin d from the sun vitamin e vitamin a and vitamin k2 from animal fat egg yolks liver grass-fed animal fat will provide all of those things then you may develop cavities from eating sugar when i was a vegan i was eating food every day and i went to the dentist and they told me i had seven cavities i didn't feel them at the time i said uh i don't wanna get those filled i didn't know that i was gonna not be a vegan in a few months but guess what i have zero cavities now so clearly changing my diet was a major thing for me and those cavities did not progress and i eat a massive amount of fruit right now if you've seen me in my stories i do not fear fruit but you know for fructose i've talked about that on previous podcasts at length so i do not believe that fruit is the only variable in the formation of tooth cavities i believe that is a connection or a contextual variable with the fat soluble vitamin deficiency and we see that hypothesis corroborating in studies of hunter-gatherer tribes and indigenous people who are eating lots of fat soluble vitamins vitamin k2 was western prices x factor this fat soluble nutrient found in butter egg yolks liver raw milk etc that i think is the key to a healthy mouth now when i was growing up did i have a lot of that nope wasn't because my mom and dad wanted to deprive me of it they just didn't understand the value of those foods in a child's life i understand the value of those foods in a child's life and if i ever have kids you can better believe my kids will basically be swimming in egg yolks liver meat meat fat and probably some butter and hopefully

chunks.title: Skepticism of Fluoride Solutions
chunks.summary: The speaker expresses skepticism towards fluoride as a solution for cavities, arguing that while it can reduce decay, it should not be a substitute for proper nutrition. They advocate for feeding children nutrient-rich foods to eliminate the need for fluoride treatments.
chunks.transcript: there is a pretty clear body of literature to suggest that putting fluoride in the water does decrease at rates of tooth decay and rates of dental cavities fluoride can form hydroxyapatite just like calcium can form hydroxyapatite in the tooth enamel and that is resistant to cavities now i don't believe that we need to be doing that with fluoridated water if our children are well nourished in areas of the country where the children are not well nourished is it a positive health intervention for public health well you decide what you want to do for yourself and your children i don't think i would do it for my family because i would feed them the fat soluble nutrient-rich foods and therefore would avoid the need for fluoride in the water i think fluoride is a band-aid in the
